children.value: This chapter documents the design decisions that apply across the behavioral data analysis pipeline. The model chapters
cross-reference back to the corresponding section here.

children.label: Treatment of the ordered condition factor

children.value: The instruction-condition factor takes three values

children.html: {1,2,3}\{1, 2, 3\}{1,2,3}

children.value: along a single epistemic-uncertainty axis: each step from condition 1 to 3 reduces explicit information about the task structure. We encode this via

condition_Q1,2,3={+16,−26,+16}≈{+0.408,−0.816,+0.408}.\text{condition\_Q}_{1,2,3} = \big\{+\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}, -\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{6}}, +\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{6}}\big\} \approx \{+0.408, -0.816, +0.408\}.condition_Q1,2,3​={+6​1​,−6​2​,+6​1​}≈{+0.408,−0.816,+0.408}.

children.value: effect of moving from condition 1 to condition 3. A coefficient of

children.html: βL\beta_LβL​

children.value: on this contrast corresponds to a per-unit-contrast effect; the predicted difference between condition 1 and condition 3 on the response scale is

children.html: 2⋅βL≈1.414⋅βL\sqrt{2} \cdot \beta_L \approx 1.414 \cdot \beta_L2​⋅βL​≈1.414⋅βL​

children.value: indicates that condition 2’s mean is meaningfully different from a linear interpolation between conditions 1 and 3. This is theoretically secondary, but it serves as control, as it absorbs any non-monotonic pattern (e.g. condition 2 being systematically different because vague hints prime attention without resolving uncertainty), preventing such patterns from leaking into the linear contrast estimate.
How this design translates to the model implementations

Episodes are 16–24 trials long by design (see
). This jitter creates three distinct statistical risks if not handled deliberately.

children.label: Risk 1: Imbalanced cells from raw trial_in_episode

children.value: Pooling across episodes when fitting an interaction with raw
over-weights late-trial information from longer episodes. Concretely, “trial_in_episode = 20” exists in 24-trial episodes but not in 16-trial ones, and any interaction with
will silently weight late-position information from longer episodes more heavily.
This is resolved by normalisation. All within-episode dynamics models use the

Risk 2: Episode-length confound with within-episode metrics

children.label: Risk 2: Episode-length confound with within-episode metrics

children.value: Within-episode means of consolidation-style DVs (e.g. circular variance, accuracy) are mechanically lower in longer episodes, simply because more trials are spent at the asymptotic low-variance / low-error end of the consolidation curve.
This is taken into account by including
(centred episode length) as a fixed-effect covariate in all within-episode dynamics models.

. Per-participant centring (rather than grand-mean centring) prevents cross-participant variation in mean episode count from biasing the slope estimate when episode coverage is uneven.

Centring matters especially for interaction interpretation (when one term in an interaction is uncentered, the other term’s “main effect” coefficient is its effect at the
value of the first term, not at the mean. So centring makes interaction coefficients interpretable as effects at the marginal mean)
(This can be a mess. So I included it here explicitly to note how we treat the circular data equally over all analysis steps - this is also a reminder for myself to keep it consistent)

children.value: , with 0 meaning perfectly concentrated and 1 meaning uniformly distributed.

Mixed-effects models are fit through a four-tier fallback ladder. The motivation is to increase robustness by also allowing for adaptive complexity increase as soon as more data is available. At small N or with rich random-effect structures, the most-preferred fit may not be identifiable (and the script must progress gracefully rather than crash). Every fit records which tier was actually used in the model’s
Tier 1: full random-effect structure with REML

children.label: Tier 1: full random-effect structure with REML

children.value: The requested random-effect structure is fit via
with REML estimation. The fit is rejected and a fallback is triggered if either:

children.html: <10−8< 10^{-8}<10−8

children.value: . We test on the symmetric part

children.html: (Σre+Σre⊤)/2(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\text{re}} + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\text{re}}^\top)/2(Σre​+Σre⊤​)/2

children.value: Especially the singularity check is important to avoid understating uncertainty. With a singular cov. matrix,
will return a “converged” result whose RE covariance is zero or rank-deficient (and naively reporting from it would understate uncertainty).
If Tier 1 fails, the same fixed-effect formula is fit with the random-effect structure simplified to
. Same convergence and singularity checks apply.
If Tier 2 fails (meaning even the simple random intercept has near-zero variance) the model is fit with
(CR1, the small-sample-corrected sandwich estimator), clustered on

This is the principled fallback when between-participant variance is genuinely near-zero (so a random intercept is unidentified) but trials remain nested within participants (so naive OLS SEs would be wrong). The fixed-effect estimates are unbiased; the SEs are correctly inflated to account for within-participant correlation.
Last resort if even cluster-robust OLS fails to fit (but this is never reached, even with the small piloting N = 17).
The design matrix is constructed via

and its rank is checked against the column count. A rank-deficient design (rank < ncol) is recorded as a warning in the diagnostics file (this makes multicollinearity-driven failures legible from the diagnostics output rather than buried in cryptic optimisation warnings).
Cluster-robust SEs in the survival framework

children.label: Cluster-robust SEs in the survival framework

children.value: The cluster-robust analogue in M_SURV is the Lin-Wei robust variance estimator (Lin & Wei, 1989), invoked via
. This is conceptually parallel to the OLS cluster-robust SEs but adapted for the partial likelihood setting. See
The fallback ladder may end on a fit that has no random-effects machinery (cluster-robust OLS or plain OLS). The
If present, returns the BLUPs as a DataFrame, but only if the random-effect covariance is non-singular (per the eigenvalue check).
otherwise, with the calling code skipping the BLUP write rather than producing misleading per-participant estimates.
is a property defined on the wrapper instance, not the type. The implementation uses
Variance partitioning notes for the binomial model (M7)

children.label: Variance partitioning notes for the binomial model (M7)

children.value: . Consequently, M7 uses a tier ladder distinct from M1–M6:
, which provides true subject-specific random intercepts (via variational approximation). Posterior means and SDs are returned; for cross-model reporting consistency, we compute Wald-style

children.value: GEE with binomial family and exchangeable working correlation
is a population-averaged estimator, used as fallback if the Bayesian VB fit fails.
The Bayesian-VB fit is preferred because it provides participant-level random effects analogous to the BLUPs from M1–M6, allowing M7 to contribute to the
analysis (GEE estimates are population-averaged and do not yield per-participant random effects)
The framework declares the following confirmatory set of focal coefficients across the seven primary GLMMs:
main effect in M1, M3, M5, M7
in M3 and M4 (the focal Factor 1 interactions)

within the confirmatory set is the planned default for analyses on full data. Supporting analyses (other coefficients in the same models, the bootstrap factor-loading stability, the Kaplan–Meier log-rank tests) are reported as
, without further multiple-comparison correction (this split is intended to preserve power on the focal hypotheses without underplaying the descriptive findings - but
The pipeline is designed to be fully reproducible from raw participant CSVs alone:
produces the trial-level and episode-level datasets and runs M1–M7
consumes the trial-level dataset and runs M_CP and M_SURV
consumes the BLUP CSVs and runs the factor analysis

All random-number-using analyses (parallel analysis, bootstrap stability, Kaplan–Meier confidence interval computation) accept seeds for full reproducibility, default seeds are encoded in the scripts.

Lin, D. Y., & Wei, L. J. (1989). The robust inference for the Cox proportional hazards model.
